questions.question: How many unique IPv4 addresses exist in total?
questions.explanation: IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses, so 2^32 = 4,294,967,296 unique addresses exist. This scarcity (4.3 billion sounds like a lot, but many are reserved) is exactly why IPv6 with 128-bit addresses was created. IPv6's 2^128 is the 340 undecillion figure.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the subnet mask?
options: It encrypts the address so routers cannot read it
It separates the network portion of an address from the host portion
It assigns a default gateway to a host
questions.explanation: The subnet mask (or prefix length) defines which bits of an IP address identify the network and which bits identify the individual host. A router performs a bitwise AND between a destination address and the mask to extract the network address, which it then looks up in its routing table.

questions.question: Given address 192.168.5.130 and mask 255.255.255.128 (/25), what is the network address?
A /26 subnet has how many usable host addresses?
questions.explanation: A /26 leaves 32 - 26 = 6 host bits. 2^6 = 64 total addresses. We subtract 2: one for the network address (all host bits 0) and one for the broadcast address (all host bits 1). 64 - 2 = 62 usable hosts. This subtraction of 2 is a constant rule for any subnet regardless of prefix length.

questions.question: Why did address classes become obsolete and get replaced by CIDR?
options: Classes used hexadecimal notation which routers could not parse
Classes forced fixed-size allocations (8, 16, or 24 network bits) that wasted enormous numbers of addresses
Classes required every host to know the class of every other host
CIDR added encryption that classes lacked
questions.explanation: Classful addressing had only three sizes: Class A (/8, ~16 million hosts), Class B (/16, ~65,000 hosts), and Class C (/24, 254 hosts). If you needed 300 hosts you were forced to take a Class B, wasting ~65,000 addresses. CIDR allows any prefix length, so you can allocate a /23 (510 hosts) or a /22 (1,022 hosts) — exactly the right size.
